You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? - React Router: The Basics - (24)

For really small projects, everything we've learned in the earlier chapters is honestly more than enough. But real-world projects don't work that way — you can't get by with just the basics, because as your project grows, the pages get more and more complex. At that point you need a tool to help you manage all those pages, and that tool is React Router.
So what is React Router? Before I get into it, I want to quickly cover one concept first: the so-called SPA (Single Page Application).
And what's an SPA? An SPA is a web development pattern...

Okay, let me stop right there. That description was starting to get a little too fancy and formal, so let me just keep it simple. Basically, it means you stay on the same page from start to finish. What do I mean by "the same page"? I'll explain as we go.
Like I mentioned, SPA stands for "Single Page Application," which basically means you stay on
the entire time. So if a site is built using the SPA pattern, the URL in your browser's address bar might look something like this:
That string is an SPA page formed by simulating backend routing through JavaScript. You might be wondering — wait, where did
, so it gets left out. Some sites default to
instead, and which page is the default is something you set on the server. But the default is usually
Before SPAs became popular, people built sites the MPA way (Multi Page Application). With MPAs, the address bar looks like this:
is one way the frontend simulates backend routing. You
adjust it to look more like an MPA-style route, but that requires coordination with the backend, so in SPA development people mostly stick with the
simulation. This mode is also called
This isn't really the part we need to dig into, but if you want to go deeper, you can check out
this earlier post I wrote on SPA, CSR, SSR, MPA, and SSG terminology

So now that we've got a basic feel for what an SPA is, React Router is the core piece that helps us pull this off. Go ahead and open up any project — the Vite TodoList project we built in an earlier chapter works fine — and run the following command in your terminal to install React Router:
project I built earlier as the demo. Feel free to download it now, or just spin up a new project.
First step: open up App.jsx and import
is basically a component, so all you need to do is wrap your entire

import React from 'react'
import { HashRouter } from "react-router-dom";
import ReactDOM from 'react-dom/client'
import App from './App'
import './index.css'

ReactDOM.createRoot(document.getElementById('root')).render(
  <React.StrictMode>
    <HashRouter>
      <App />
    </HashRouter>
  </React.StrictMode>
)
Congrats! You've successfully brought React Router into the project. Now we can start using it.

No time to waste — let's pull the
that was sitting in App.jsx out into its own component, say ToDoList.jsx, and change App.jsx to this:
Next, open main.jsx, import ToDoList.jsx, and use it together with

import React from 'react'
import { HashRouter, Route } from "react-router-dom";
import ReactDOM from 'react-dom/client'
import App from './App'
import './index.css'

import ToDoList from "./TodoList";

ReactDOM.createRoot(document.getElementById('root')).render(
  <React.StrictMode>
    <HashRouter>
      <Route path="/" element={ <App /> } />
      <Route path="/todolist" element={ <ToDoList /> } />
    </HashRouter>
  </React.StrictMode>
)
to start the project and head to
in your browser, you'll find the screen is completely blank, and the console throws this error:
Uncaught Error: A <Route> is only ever to be used as the child of <Routes> element, never rendered directly. Please wrap your <Route> in a <Routes>.
I did that on purpose to show you a wrong way of writing it. In short,
, so the correct version has to be written like this:

import React from 'react'
import { HashRouter, Route, Routes } from "react-router-dom";
import ReactDOM from 'react-dom/client'
import App from './App'
import './index.css'
import ToDoList from "./TodoList";

ReactDOM.createRoot(document.getElementById('root')).render(
  <React.StrictMode>
    <HashRouter>
      <Routes>
        <Route path="/" element={ <App /> } />
        <Route path="/todolist" element={ <ToDoList /> } />
      </Routes>
    </HashRouter>
  </React.StrictMode>
)
, you should see a pretty empty screen with one lonely little word — "App" — sitting there all by itself. That's totally normal, because that's just what the home page is.

For tiny projects, everything we've covered so far is honestly more than enough. But real projects don't work like that — as the app grows, your pages get more and more complex, and at some point you need a tool to help you manage them. That tool is React Router. In this post I'll introduce what it is, walk through SPA basics, and get a minimal router up and running.
